result.markdown:
--- title: "Data Loaders — Feeding Reactive OJS from Any Language" subtitle: "The Quarto equivalent of Observable Framework's data loaders: compute on the back-end, visualise on the front-end" engine: jupyter format: html: toc: true toc-depth: 3 code-fold: show --- ::: {.callout-tip} ## One idea, three moving parts [Observable Framework](https://github.com/observablehq/framework) popularised the **data loader** pattern: a script in *any* language (Python, R, SQL, shell) produces a static data snapshot at build time, and the front-end loads it instantly with no live server. Quarto expresses the same idea natively: 1. A **`{python}` / `{r}` / `{bash}` chunk** does the heavy analysis at render time. 2. **`ojs_define()`** (or a written-out file + **`FileAttachment`**) hands the result to the browser. 3. A **`{ojs}` cell** consumes it and stays fully reactive. The expensive computation runs once, during `quarto render`; the published page is then pure static HTML that reacts instantly. ::: --- # 1. Live pattern — Python loader → `ojs_define` → reactive OJS The Python chunk below simulates a factor-level assay dataset and passes it straight into the Observable runtime with `ojs_define()`. ::: {#python-loader .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import numpy as np, pandas as pd rng = np.random.default_rng(7) n = 200 severity = rng.choice(["Severe", "Moderate", "Mild"], size=n, p=[0.34, 0.33, 0.33]) baseline = np.where(severity == "Severe", rng.uniform(0, 1, n), np.where(severity == "Moderate", rng.uniform(1, 5, n), rng.uniform(5, 40, n))) age = rng.integers(4, 75, n) abr = np.clip(np.where(severity == "Severe", 6, np.where(severity == "Moderate", 2.5, 0.6)) + rng.normal(0, 2, n), 0, None).round() assays = pd.DataFrame({ "severity": severity, "baseline": baseline.round(2), "age": age, "abr": abr.astype(int), }) # Hand the whole table to Observable JS under the name `assays`. ojs_define(assays = assays) assays.head() ``` ::: {#python-loader-1 .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html} ``` ::: ::: {#python-loader-2 .cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ```{=html}
severity baseline age abr
0 Moderate 2.32 47 2
1 Mild 31.44 74 2
2 Mild 16.78 46 2
3 Severe 0.19 15 5
4 Severe 0.57 69 7
``` ::: ::: An OJS cell now reads that exact table and draws it — the analysis ran once in Python at render time, the chart is pure client-side SVG: ```{ojs} //| echo: false // `assays` arrives from Python as a column-oriented object → transpose to rows. rowsFromPython = transpose(assays) ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false Plot.plot({ title: `${rowsFromPython.length} factor-level assays supplied by the Python loader`, width: 760, height: 340, grid: true, x: {label: "Baseline factor level (%) →"}, y: {label: "↑ Annualised bleed rate"}, color: {legend: true}, marks: [ Plot.dot(rowsFromPython, {x: "baseline", y: "abr", fill: "severity", r: 4.5, fillOpacity: 0.8, tip: true}), Plot.linearRegressionY(rowsFromPython, {x: "baseline", y: "abr"}) ] }) ``` ::: {.callout-note} `ojs_define()` serialises a pandas/R data frame as **columns**. Observable's built-in `transpose()` helper flips it back to the array-of-rows shape that `Plot` and `Inputs.table` expect. **Why no slider here?** Mixing an `ojs_define` (server → client) with an interactive `viewof` (client input) on the *same* page makes Quarto emit a Shiny server binding, which needs `server: shiny`. For static publishing, keep loaders and interactive widgets on separate pages — the live sliders live on the [Inputs gallery](observable-inputs.qmd). ::: --- # 2. Live pattern — `FileAttachment` reads a vendored CSV No back-end language at all: a static CSV in the project's `data/` folder, parsed in the browser. This is the closest analogue to a Framework file-based data loader. ```{ojs} penguins = FileAttachment("../data/penguins.csv").csv({typed: true}) ``` ```{ojs} //| echo: false Plot.plot({ title: `Palmer penguins — ${penguins.length} rows loaded via FileAttachment`, width: 760, height: 360, grid: true, color: {legend: true}, x: {label: "Culmen length (mm) →"}, y: {label: "↑ Flipper length (mm)"}, marks: [ Plot.dot(penguins, {x: "culmen_length_mm", y: "flipper_length_mm", fill: "species", tip: true}) ] }) ``` `FileAttachment` also parses `.json()`, `.text()`, `.arrayBuffer()`, `.image()` and `.arrow()` — so the same mechanism loads Arrow tables, GeoJSON, or images. --- # 3. Reference patterns for other back-ends These are the loader idioms for the remaining languages, adapted from the [`data-loader-examples`](https://github.com/observablehq/data-loader-examples) repository. Shown as listings because a single Quarto document runs one execution engine at a time — copy them into a knitr- or shell-engined page to run live. ### R loader (knitr engine) ```r #| label: r-loader library(dplyr) recovery <- tibble( day = 0:28, platelets = 20 + 380 / (1 + exp(-0.30 * (0:28 - 14))) ) ojs_define(recovery = recovery) # → available as `recovery` in {ojs} ``` ### Shell / SQL loader (writes a file the page then attaches) A `bash` chunk (or a standalone data-loader script) can shell out to `duckdb`, `curl`, `jq` or any CLI and emit a data file: ```bash # data/labs.csv is regenerated at render time duckdb -c "COPY ( SELECT severity, avg(baseline) AS mean_baseline FROM read_csv_auto('raw/assays.csv') GROUP BY severity ) TO 'data/labs.csv' (HEADER, DELIMITER ',');" ``` ```{{ojs}} labs = FileAttachment("../data/labs.csv").csv({typed: true}) ``` ### JavaScript loader (fetch + transform at render, cache with `freeze`) ```js // A Node data loader that fetches and reshapes an API response. const res = await fetch("https://example.org/api/registry.json"); const data = (await res.json()).map(d => ({id: d.id, abr: +d.annualBleeds})); process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify(data)); // → data/registry.json ``` --- ::: {.callout-important} ## Why this is the powerful bit Quarto's `execute: freeze: auto` (set in `_quarto.yml`) means each loader runs **only when its source changes**. The committed `_freeze/` cache lets continuous-publishing CI rebuild the site *without* re-installing Python, R or database drivers — the reactive front-end ships as static assets. Heavy analysis stays reproducible; the reader gets instant interactivity. See the [Inputs gallery](observable-inputs.qmd) and [Plot showcase](observable-plot.qmd) for what to build on top of the loaded data. :::
